retrieved_docs.title: Acute Cardiac Tamponade -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiac tamponade occurs when excessive fluid accumulates in the pericardium, in turn compressing the heart and restricting the filling of the cardiac chambers and ultimately causing hypotension and cardiac arrest. This activity reviews the etiology, signs and symptoms, diagnosis, and management of pericarditis and cardiac tamponade and reviews the role of the interprofessional team in managing this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: Cardiac Tamponade -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiac tamponade is a medical emergency that takes place when abnormal amounts of fluid accumulate in the pericardial sac compressing the heart and leading to a decrease in cardiac output and shock. This activity describes the evaluation and management of cardiac tamponade and reviews the role of the interprofessional team in improving care for this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: Cardiac Tamponade -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: The treatment of cardiac tamponade is the removal of pericardial fluid to help relieve the pressure surrounding the heart. This can be done by performing a needle pericardiocentesis at the bedside, performed either using traditional landmark technique in a sub-xiphoid window or using a point-of-care echo to guide needle placement in real-time.  Often the removal of the first small amounts of fluid can make a large improvement in hemodynamics, but leaving a catheter within the pericardium can allow for further drainage. [10] [11] [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Chest Pain -- Treatment / Management -- Pericardial tamponade
retrieved_docs.content: Bedside ultrasound is useful for establishing a diagnosis. A fluid bolus may be used as a temporizing measure. Needle pericardiotomy or pericardial window to relieve pressure inside the pericardial sack. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Cardiac Tamponade -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Surgical options include creating a pericardial window or removing the pericardium. Emergency department resuscitative thoracotomy and the opening of the pericardial sac is a therapy that can be used in traumatic arrests with suspected or confirmed cardiac tamponade. These options are preferable to needle pericardiocentesis for traumatic pericardial effusions. Volume resuscitation and pressor support may be helpful; however, these are temporizing measures that should be performed while preparing for definitive treatment with one of the above procedures.

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Cardiac Tamponade -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Physician assistants, nurse practitioners, and nurses also play a vital role in managing patients with acute pericarditis and cardiac tamponade. Their role includes medication adjustment and follow-up at the pericardial clinic. They not only co-manage patients with the physicians by monitoring patients’ symptoms and inflammatory markers but also help in contacting patients by phone, titrating dosages of medications, and arranging follow-up visits with an NP/PA in 3 months and with the physician every 6 months.

retrieved_docs.title: Cardiac Tamponade -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Pleural effusion Pneumothorax Pulmonary embolism Constrictive pericarditis Heart failure Shock

retrieved_docs.title: Cardiac Tamponade -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnosis of pericardial effusion with tamponade can be difficult using clinical exam alone. A point-of-care echocardiogram can be very useful to confirm the diagnosis and determine the need for intervention. Remember, tamponade is ultimately a clinical diagnosis requiring both hemodynamically unstable patient and pericardial effusion.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 2 -- Community 903
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiovascular monitoring encompasses a broad range of diagnostic and invasive techniques aimed at assessing cardiac function and systemic hemodynamics. It includes electrocardiogram monitoring such as the “Electrocardiogram monitoring, 10 hour portrait” and specialized fetal electrocardiogram, as well as comprehensive monitoring of ECG, arterial pressure, blood gases, and cardiac output. Invasive arterial monitoring is achieved through percutaneous insertion of catheters into various vessels, including the left carotid artery, left temporal artery, right axillary artery, right carotid artery, right femoral artery, and right temporal artery, each providing direct pressure and flow data from the respective arterial sites. Cardiovascular monitoring also incorporates transesophageal echocardiographic monitoring, which offers real‑time imaging of cardiac structures via the esophagus, and hemodynamické monitorování (hemodynamic monitoring), which evaluates overall circulatory status. Together, these modalities provide clinicians with detailed information for the diagnosis, management, and therapeutic guidance of patients with cardiovascular and systemic conditions.

Overall, the knowledge base emphasizes coordinated vascular access strategies that integrate precise arterial catheter placement for real‑time pressure and cardiac output assessment with catheter‑based delivery of therapeutic agents, thereby facilitating both diagnostic monitoring and targeted treatment during major surgical procedures and broader patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 2 -- Community 1053
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiovascular monitoring comprises a broad array of diagnostic and invasive techniques used to assess cardiac function and hemodynamics in diverse clinical settings. It includes continuous electrocardiogram (ECG) surveillance such as a 10‑hour portrait ECG recording, fetal ECG monitoring, and intra‑operative ECG monitoring performed during major surgery. Comprehensive monitoring protocols may combine ECG data with arterial pressure measurements, blood‑gas analysis, and cardiac output assessment to provide an integrated view of a patient’s cardiovascular status. A key focus of cardiovascular monitoring is the percutaneous insertion of arterial catheters for real‑time pressure and waveform analysis. Catheters may be placed in the left or right femoral artery, the left or right radial artery, and the right temporal artery, each offering reliable access for arterial pressure monitoring and blood sampling. Transesophageal echocardiographic monitoring represents a specialized form of cardiovascular monitoring that utilizes an esophageal probe to obtain high‑resolution images of cardiac structures and function, complementing surface ECG and invasive pressure measurements. Together, these modalities enable clinicians to detect arrhythmias, assess myocardial performance, guide therapeutic interventions, and improve patient outcomes across peri‑operative, obstetric, and critical‑care environments.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 2 -- Community 861
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiovascular monitoring encompasses a broad range of diagnostic and invasive techniques used to assess cardiac function and hemodynamics. It includes specific monitoring modalities such as Electrocardiogram monitoring with a 10‑hour portrait, Fetal electrocardiogram, Monitoring of ECG and pressure during major surgery, Monitoring of ECG at surgery, and comprehensive Monitoring of ECG, pressure, blood gases and cardiac output. In addition to these non‑invasive and semi‑invasive approaches, cardiovascular monitoring places particular emphasis on the percutaneous insertion of catheters for arterial pressure monitoring, targeting the left axillary artery, left femoral artery, left radial artery, right radial artery, and right temporal artery. These arterial catheterizations provide continuous waveform and pressure data essential for intra‑operative and critical‑care management. Furthermore, Transesophageal echocardiographic monitoring is classified as a type of cardiovascular monitoring, offering real‑time imaging of cardiac structures and function. Together, these interconnected methods form an integrated strategy for detailed cardiovascular assessment and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 931
retrieved_docs.content: Arterial cannulation with cardiac‑output measurement is classified as a form of other vascular catheterization that does not involve direct instrumentation of the heart and is performed by percutaneously inserting an arterial catheter for continuous hemodynamic monitoring. The procedure can be carried out in a wide range of arterial sites—including the left and right axillary arteries, left and right carotid arteries, left and right femoral arteries, left and right radial arteries, and left and right temporal arteries—providing routes for real‑time assessment of arterial pressure, flow, and cardiac performance. Within the broader domain of cardiovascular monitoring, clinicians integrate data from electrocardiogram (ECG) monitoring, arterial pressure recordings, blood‑gas analysis, and cardiac‑output measurements, and they may also employ transesophageal echocardiographic monitoring via an esophageal probe to obtain detailed ultrasound images of the heart and great vessels. Together, these invasive arterial catheterizations and imaging techniques enable comprehensive, continuous surveillance of circulatory function, guiding therapeutic decisions, enhancing patient safety, and improving outcomes in peri‑operative and critical‑care settings.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 2 -- Community 953
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiovascular monitoring is a comprehensive clinical practice that includes systemic arterial pressure monitoring as a subordinate component. It focuses on the percutaneous insertion of arterial catheters for continuous hemodynamic assessment, with specific techniques described for the left and right axillary arteries, left and right femoral arteries, left and right radial arteries, left and right temporal arteries, and the right carotid artery. Each of these catheter‑based approaches is employed to obtain accurate arterial pressure data and to support real‑time cardiovascular evaluation. In addition to arterial catheterization, cardiovascular monitoring is classified as a form of transesophageal echocardiographic monitoring and is also considered a type of hemodynamic monitoring (hemodynamické monitorování). Together, these modalities provide clinicians with detailed information on systemic arterial pressures and overall cardiac function, facilitating precise diagnosis, therapeutic decision‑making, and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 2 -- Community 1103
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiovascular monitoring encompasses a range of specialized techniques used to assess cardiac function and related physiological parameters in various clinical settings. It includes specific monitoring modalities such as Electrocardiogram monitoring with a 10‑hour portrait, Fetal electrocardiogram, Monitoring of ECG and pressure during major surgery, Monitoring of ECG at surgery, and comprehensive Monitoring of ECG, pressure, blood gases, and cardiac output. The focus of cardiovascular monitoring also extends to the percutaneous insertion of arterial catheters for continuous measurement, with particular attention to catheter placement in the left carotid artery, left femoral artery, right radial artery, and right temporal artery. Additionally, Transesophageal echocardiographic monitoring is classified as a form of cardiovascular monitoring, providing detailed imaging of cardiac structures via the esophagus. Together, these interconnected methods enable precise real‑time evaluation of cardiac and hemodynamic status, supporting informed decision‑making and optimal patient care during surgical procedures, fetal assessment, and critical care management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 1024
retrieved_docs.content: Arterial cannulation with cardiac output is classified as a form of other vascular catheterization and is encompassed by the broader category of percutaneous insertion of arterial catheters for monitoring. This technique can be performed in the left and right axillary, carotid, femoral, radial, and temporal arteries, providing continuous cardiovascular monitoring that includes electrocardiogram (ECG) monitoring, 10‑hour portrait recordings, fetal ECG, intra‑operative ECG surveillance, as well as simultaneous measurement of pressure, blood gases, and cardiac output. The percutaneous arterial access enables real‑time pressure waveforms, blood sampling, and hemodynamic data essential for precise management in surgery, critical care, and other high‑risk settings. Cardiovascular monitoring is also considered a subtype of transesophageal echocardiographic (TEE) monitoring, allowing integration of arterial catheter data with TEE imaging to deliver comprehensive structural and functional cardiac assessment, thereby supporting detailed patient evaluation and timely therapeutic decision‑making.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 2 -- Community 968
retrieved_docs.content: Electrocardiogram monitoring, including 10‑hour portrait, fetal electrocardiogram, intra‑operative ECG and pressure monitoring, combined ECG, pressure, blood‑gas and cardiac‑output monitoring, and transesophageal echocardiographic monitoring, are diagnostic and monitoring procedures performed directly on the heart (srdce), as is myocardial imaging, underscoring the heart as the primary anatomic site for cardiac imaging studies. Therapeutic device interventions such as implantation of a rate‑responsive cardiac single‑chamber device, initial implantation of a cardiac single‑chamber device, removal of a cardiac pacemaker with replacement by a single‑chamber device (rate‑responsive), removal of pacemaker electrode leads without replacement, replacement of a cardiac pacemaker device with a dual‑chamber device, and the initial insertion of a transvenous lead (electrode) into the ventricle (device kardiostimulátor) involve the heart indirectly and are classified under the comprehensive procedural category “Insertion; revision; replacement; removal of cardiac pacemaker or cardioverter/defibrillator.” This category serves as the overarching classification for all insertion, revision, replacement, and removal activities related to cardiac pacemakers and cardioverter‑defibrillators, including specific actions such as lead‑only removal and dual‑chamber device replacement. Anatomically, the heart is the associated site of bacterial myocarditis (التهاب عضل القلب الجرثومي), a condition situated within the broader groups of peri‑, endo‑, and myocarditis and cardiomyopathy, and it can manifest clinically as a cardiac murmur (نفخة قلبية). Collectively, these relationships illustrate the central role of the heart as the site for a wide spectrum of diagnostic monitoring, imaging, and device‑based therapeutic procedures, its involvement in infectious and inflammatory cardiac diseases, and its clinical signs, all organized within standardized procedural classifications.

retrieved_docs.content: Cardiovascular monitoring encompasses a broad set of procedures and techniques used to assess cardiac activity and related physiologic parameters. It includes specific child modalities such as Electrocardiogram monitoring, 10 hour portrait, Fetal electrocardiogram, Monitoring of ECG at surgery, and Monitoring of ECG, pressure, blood gases and cardiac output, each providing targeted information about heart rhythm and circulatory status. The focus of Cardiovascular monitoring also involves the percutaneous insertion of arterial catheters for continuous measurement, with detailed approaches for catheter placement into the left femoral artery, left temporal artery, and right axillary artery, as well as a general emphasis on percutaneous insertion of arterial catheter for monitoring. Additionally, Transesophageal echocardiographic monitoring is classified as a type of Cardiovascular monitoring. Together, these interconnected modalities enable clinicians to obtain real‑time, comprehensive data essential for patient management during surgery, critical care, and obstetric monitoring.

It comprises systemic arterial pressure monitoring, continuous electrocardiogram monitoring (including 10‑hour portrait, fetal ECG, ECG‑pressure monitoring during major surgery, and combined ECG‑pressure‑blood‑gas‑cardiac‑output monitoring), and transesophageal echocardiographic monitoring. Hemodynamic monitoring is a subtype of “procedures on blood vessels” and is conceptually linked to Swan‑Ganz catheterization for advanced pulmonary‑artery pressure assessment. Catheter‑based therapeutic procedures form a related family that includes catheter ablation of cardiac tissue, coronary vascular interventions (“رأب وعائي تاجي”), pulmonary‑artery catheterization (“قثطرة الشريان الرئوي”), and irrigation of vascular catheters; all share principles of intravascular access, device manipulation, and physiological monitoring.
